prompt:
You are synthesizing a JTBD switch-interview round across 3 captured interviews. Produce: (1) per-interview synthesis (timeline, four forces with verbatim quotes and interview ID attribution); (2) cross-interview cluster roll-up tagging each force STRONG (3+ interviews), EMERGING (2 interviews), or ANECDOTAL (1 interview); (3) PROPOSED decision block(s) ONLY for clusters strong enough to justify them (anecdotal clusters MUST NOT drive proposals); (4) a 'We do not have evidence for X' gap subsection naming any surviving team assumption not confirmed or disconfirmed.

Context: team is shipping a Supabase-backed expense-tracker SaaS. Team's prior assumption (the assumption the round is testing): 'users switch from Excel to our SaaS because of automation (auto-categorization, receipt OCR).'

Interview transcripts (compressed; the quoted lines are exact user language):

**Interview I1** (subject: freelance designer, switched 3 months ago from Excel + manual entry to a competing SaaS):
- On the moment of switch: "I was up at 2am the night before my taxes were due and I realized I'd double-counted a whole month."
- On the old solution: "Every time I added a row I was scared I'd break the formula in the totals column."
- On the new solution: "What sold me was honestly that I could just photograph a receipt and stop thinking about it."
- On what almost stopped her: "I really hesitated giving them my bank login. I still don't fully trust it."
- On habit: did not surface anything about Excel muscle memory.

**Interview I2** (subject: solo consultant, switched 6 weeks ago from Excel to a competing SaaS):
- On the moment of switch: "I'd put it off for a year. Then I billed the wrong client and lost a $400 retainer because of a row I'd accidentally dragged."
- On the old solution: "I knew the formulas in that sheet better than I knew my own apartment."
- On the new solution: "They had a one-click import that just worked. I gave it ten minutes and it was done."
- On what almost stopped him: "I genuinely worried I would lose history. My sheet had five years of data."
- On habit: "Honestly the muscle memory of keyboard-only navigation in Excel is the thing I miss the most."

**Interview I3** (subject: small-business owner, switched 1 month ago from Excel to a competing SaaS):
- On the moment of switch: "My accountant told me he'd fire me as a client if I sent him another shoebox of receipts and a sheet with broken formulas."
- On the old solution: "It took me four hours every Sunday and I always found another error the next week."
- On the new solution: "Once it was importing receipts from my email I never went back."
- On what almost stopped her: did not surface any anxiety.
- On habit: "I kept the spreadsheet open in another tab for a month before I admitted I didn't need it."

Auth model and data scope assumptions: not relevant; this is a qualitative synthesis task.
expected_output: Four-forces cluster strength MUST be: PUSH = STRONG (all 3 interviews describe an error-cost trigger and intolerable old solution); PULL = STRONG (all 3 describe a concrete onboarding moment that sold them: photograph receipt / one-click import / email import); ANXIETY = EMERGING (only I1 and I2 surfaced anxiety; I3 explicitly did not); HABIT = EMERGING (I2 and I3 named habit; I1 did not). The team's prior assumption ('automation = auto-categorization, receipt OCR') is PARTIALLY confirmed via OCR/import quotes but NOT confirmed for auto-categorization, which should land in 'We do not have evidence for X'. ANXIETY around bank-login/data-loss is the load-bearing finding the team probably did not plan for; persona should PROPOSE it as a risk in IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md ## Risks and mitigations. ANECDOTAL clusters (none in this case at the force level, though specific sub-claims like 'bank-login distrust' surface only once and must be tagged anecdotal at the sub-claim level) MUST NOT drive a PROPOSED decision.

assertions.pattern: (We do not have evidence|do not have evidence for|evidence\s+gap|gap\s+subsection)
